data.content:
All **10 tests pass**. Here's what was created in `OrderServiceTests.cs`:

| Test | Covers |
|---|---|
| `CreateOrder_WithValidItems_ReturnsOrderWithCorrectTotal` | Happy path — total, ID, timestamp, items |
| `CreateOrder_WithSingleItem_CalculatesTotalCorrectly` | Single-item total math |
| `CreateOrder_WithNullCustomerId_ThrowsArgumentNullException` | Null guard on `customerId` |
| `CreateOrder_WithNullItems_ThrowsArgumentNullException` | Null guard on `items` |
| `CreateOrder_WithEmptyItems_ThrowsInvalidOperationException` | Empty list guard + message check |
| `GetOrder_WithExistingId_ReturnsOrder` | Successful lookup |
| `GetOrder_WithNonExistentId_ReturnsNull` | Missing order returns null |
| `GetOrdersByCustomer_ReturnsOnlyMatchingOrders` | Filters by customer correctly |
| `GetOrdersByCustomer_WithNoOrders_ReturnsEmptyList` | Empty result |
| `CreateOrder_AssignsUniqueIds` | IDs are unique across orders |

The tests follow modern MSTest conventions: `sealed` class, constructor-based setup, `Assert.ThrowsExactly<T>`, descriptive `Method_Scenario_Expected` names, and no legacy anti-patterns.
